Name: spectral_stability_bounds
Title: PAC-Bayes Frameworks for Latency-Critical Inference with Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop latency-critical inference methods that provide rigorous guarantees on their performance and efficiency, while also adapting to changing conditions at test-time through implicit optimization.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization for efficient inference. However, these approaches often rely on heuristics or empirical methods, and may not provide rigorous guarantees on their performance. In contrast, our proposal leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to provide a more principled and robust approach to latency-critical inference.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to latency-critical inference that leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds. Our method provides rigorous guarantees on its performance and efficiency, while also adapting to changing conditions at test-time through implicit optimization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on a range of benchmark datasets, and show that it outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on a range of benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10. We will evaluate our method using metrics such as accuracy, inference time, and memory usage, and compare its performance to existing state-of-the-art methods. We will also conduct ablation studies to investigate the impact of different components of our method on its overall performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor for our proposal is the complexity of the PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, which may require significant computational resources to implement. Additionally, our method may not be suitable for all types of inference tasks, and may require careful tuning of its hyperparameters to achieve optimal performance.
